blurb: The classic bedding 'geranium' — bold flower balls over scallop-edged zoned leaves all summer. Frost-tender; overwinter indoors or from cuttings.
sun: Full sun (part shade in extreme heat)
water: Moderate; let the topsoil dry between waterings
seasons: Rounded zoned leaves; rounded clusters of red/pink/white/coral flowers all summer
toxic: Mildly toxic to cats, dogs & horses (ASPCA); sap may irritate skin
origin: South Africa (Cape region) — garden hybrid of P. zonale and P. inquinans
habitat: Rocky hillsides and scrubby coastal slopes of the South African Cape

care.hardiness: A tender South African hybrid hardy only to about USDA zones 10–11, so on the Front Range (Boulder, ~5b–6a) it is grown as a warm-season annual or kept alive over winter indoors — any frost kills it.[1][2]
care.planting: Wait until danger of frost is past — after mid-May on the Front Range — and night temperatures stay above ~50°F before setting plants out in the ground or in containers. In beds, give it the hottest, sunniest spot; in pots, window boxes, and hanging baskets it excels and is easiest to bring through winter. To overwinter, before the first fall frost either take stem cuttings and root them on a bright cool windowsill, lift whole plants into pots and keep them in a bright, cool (50–60°F) room, cut back and barely watered, or store dormant plants nearly bare-root in a cool, dark basement and replant in spring.[1][3]
care.sun: Full sun gives the most compact growth and heaviest bloom — give it at least 6 hours of direct sun. Under the intense high-altitude Front Range sun, a touch of afternoon shade in the hottest part of summer keeps it from scorching, but it is the sun-lovers of the three geranium types.[1][3]
care.soil: Average to organically rich, well-drained soil with a neutral to slightly alkaline pH — Boulder's alkaline clay-loam suits it once loosened and improved with compost for drainage. In containers use a free-draining potting mix; poorly drained, soggy soil leads to stem and root rot.[1]
care.water: Moderate. Water regularly but let the top inch of soil dry between waterings — geraniums resent constantly wet feet, and steady overwatering causes edema (corky water-blisters on the leaves). In the dry Front Range air, containers and hanging baskets dry fast and may need daily summer water; beds need far less.[2][3]
care.spacing: Space plants about 8–12 inches apart in beds for a continuous mound; mature plants reach roughly 1–2 ft tall and wide in a single season.[1]
care.propagation: Easiest and truest from stem cuttings: take 3–4 inch tip cuttings in mid-to-late summer (July–August), strip the lower leaves, and root them in a gritty mix — this is also the standard way to carry a favorite cultivar through winter. Many modern seed-strain zonals also grow readily from seed started indoors on a heat mat (70–75°F) in January–February for transplants by mid-May.[2][3]
care.bloom: Blooms freely from late spring through to fall frost, carrying rounded trusses of red, pink, white, coral, or salmon florets above the zoned leaves; deadheading keeps it going.[1]
care.feeding: A moderate feeder. Work a balanced fertilizer in at planting and feed container plants every 4–6 weeks through the growing season with a balanced or bloom formula; avoid heavy nitrogen, which pushes leaves at the expense of flowers.[3]
care.maintenance: Deadhead spent flower trusses by snapping the whole stalk off at its base to keep new buds coming and reduce disease. Pinch leggy stems to keep plants bushy. Before the first frost, decide its winter route (cuttings, indoor pot, or dormant storage — see When to plant out).[1][3]
care.troubles: In Boulder's drier climate it has fewer disease problems than in humid regions, but watch for botrytis (gray mold) on flowers and foliage in cool damp spells, bacterial leaf spot, and edema from overwatering — improve airflow, avoid wetting the foliage, and water at the base. Aphids, whiteflies, spider mites, and tobacco budworm (which chews flower buds) can appear; poorly drained soil causes stem and root rot.[1][3]
care.companions: Pairs well with other heat- and sun-loving container and bedding plants — petunias, calibrachoa, sweet alyssum, lantana, dusty miller, and trailing ivy geranium spilling around its upright mound.[3]

edible.summary: Not a food plant — zonal geranium is grown purely as an ornamental and is mildly toxic to pets; do not eat it.[1][2]
edible.caution: The ASPCA lists geraniums (Pelargonium spp.) as toxic to cats, dogs, and horses; the toxic principles are the essential-oil compounds geraniol and linalool, and ingestion can cause vomiting, loss of appetite, depression, and skin (contact) dermatitis. It is not part of the human diet, and handling the foliage can irritate sensitive skin. Keep plants away from grazing pets.[1][2]
